---
title: "J 298"
author: "Ava Hu"
date: "Feb. 2026"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```


```{r library}
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr) 
library(ggplot2) 
library(knitr) 
library(psych) 
library(janitor)
library(tidycensus)
library(modelr)
```
## Load your data


Everything in R needs to get loaded into a "data frame" a sort of workspace so R can deal with it.  We'll use read.csv and because our data has field names, we include this "col_names = TRUE."  We're going to load global indicator data from the Global Health Observatory. With the following columns:

 country: country name  
 code: country code  
 gni: gross National Income per capital (in US$)  
 skulspend: education expenditures per capita  
 death: death rate per 1,000  
 healthexp: health expenditures per capita  
 unemp: unemployment rate  
 water: % with clean water  
 skul: % enrolled in secondary school  
 maternal: maternal death rate per 100,000 live births  


```
ind <- read_csv('globalind.csv', col_names = TRUE) # import global indicator data
view(ind) #view indicator data
```

I called my table "ind." You could just as easily have called it Sally, but it wouldn't be as easy to remember.


To see how the file is structured, along with record count and sample records use:

```
str(ind)

```
We have 222 observations (records) and 10 columns or variables.
The first two fields - are characters, while the other four are numeric - either integer or double

### Summarizing data 

Summarize the data. Note: Use %>% (pipe symbol) to chain multiple commands. 
To compare, use ==.

The count function serves as both group by and count unlike SQL or Excel, which require two parts. 
To make the pipe symbol shift-ctrl-m or shift-cmd-m. See keyboard shortcuts tip sheet (https://support.posit.co/hc/en-us/articles/200711853-Keyboard-Shortcuts) for more info. 
Use <- to put your results in another data frame.

#Select

```
ind %>% select(country,code,gni) # select three fields

ind  %>% select(country,code,gni) %>% 
              arrange(desc(gni)) #select three fields and sort by gni descending
```

#Filter

```
filter(ind, country == "United States") # results show up in console below 

filter(ind, gni > 60000)
```

#Put it all together

```
ind %>% 
  select(country, code, gni, skulspend) %>% 
  filter(gni >= 50000) %>% 
  arrange(desc(skulspend))
```
#Practice
1. Which country has the highest healthexp?
2. How many countries have unemployment rates higher than 10?
3. Think of a question to ask on your own.


## Practice on your own. 

Use schools.csv to answer the following questions:

schools <- read_csv('schools.csv', col_names = TRUE) # import schools data

## Aggregating data (examples using the schools data):

```
schools %>% count(District) %>% arrange(desc(n))

schools %>% summarise(totpoorpct = sum(poorpct))

schools %>% summarise(sum = sum(poorpct), count = n())
```

Sum by groups:
```
schools %>%
  group_by(County) %>% 
  summarise(studsum = sum(Students)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(studsum))
````



### Descriptives

Before diving into statistics, we need to better understand our data. What does the distribution look like? Are the mean and median close? We indicate data frame and variable like this: data_frame_name$variable_name. Let's go back to the indicator data.

```
mean(ind$gni)
median(ind$gni) 
```
Uh, oh -- We're not getting what I thought we should get. That's because there is missing data in the list of indicators by country. We need to add "na.rm=TRUE" to tell R to ignore the null values.

```
mean(ind$gni, na.rm = TRUE)
median(ind$gni,  na.rm = TRUE) 
```


Doing those individually is a lot of work. The 'psych' package we loaded has a handy function called 'describe'. 

```
describe(ind)
```
The first 2 fields are strings, so ignore the output for those. 

Once we've had a look at the data, we can explore it to see if there are any relationships.


### Correlations

Let's run some correlations looking at the relationship between skul (secondary school enrollment) and skulspend (spending on schools). We add "use="pairwise.complete.obs" to tell R to ignore missing data between pairs:

```
cor(ind$skul,ind$skulspend, use="pairwise.complete.obs")
```

One of the most useful stats tools is the correlation matrix, where you look at every variable against every other variable. 

In this case, let's look at columns 3 through 10. 

```
cor(ind[3:10], use="pairwise.complete.obs")
```
That's a lot of digits. If you'd like to round the output, wrap it in the round command. I'm using 2 decimal places.

```
round(cor(ind[3:10], use="pairwise.complete.obs"),2)
```

The strongest correlation is between gni and healthexp.


### Visualize your data: Histograms and scatter plots

To see the distribution of your data, draw a histogram.

```
hist(ind$gni)
hist(ind$healthexp)
```

Our results have a positive skew, meaning lots of values on the low end and a long tail to the right. In some analysis you may want to transform the results to get a stronger model.

For example, taking the log10 of variables that are positively skewed can normalize them to bolster your analysis. It is completely reasonable to do this, but it makes it more challenging to explain to readers.

hist(log10(ind$gni))
hist(log10(ind$healthexp))

To see the relationship between two variables, make a scatter plot. The independent variable is the X (listed first) and the dependent variable is the Y axis (listed second).

```
plot(ind$gni, ind$healthexp)
```


If you want to look at scatterplots for multiple variables, use the pairs command:
```
pairs(~ gni + healthexp + skul, data = ind)
```

### Practice with the schools data

1. Descriptives
2. Histograms
3. Correlation matrix
4. Scatterplot(s)

### Regression

Once you've identified the variables with the strongest relationship, a regression shows how strongly two or more variables are related and helps you describe the relationship. We're sticking with a basic linear model, but there are many more types of regression.

In R we use a linear model (lm) regression, but we have to do it in two steps. The first creates the model.

lm(dependent_variable ~ independent_variable, data=filename)

In terms of X and Y, X is the dependent variable and Y is the independent variable.

In this case, the healthexp is the dependent variable because we think that it depends on the gni. 
```
lm(healthexp ~ gni, data=ind)
```
We get two coefficients, the Intercept is -210.3 and gni is .107.
We have to save our model in a data frame to get the rest of the stats we need using the summary command. Let's call it pct_poor_model

```
ind_mod1 <- lm(formula = healthexp ~ gni, data = ind)
summary(ind_mod1)
```

Let's look at the results:


Start with the R-squared/adjusted R-squared: 0.87

This means that 87% of the change in health expenditures is explained by the gross national income. We know the R-squared is statistically significant because of the p-value that's well below 0.05. 
FYI: To get rid of scientific notation, we can use the command options(scipen=999).

We know that health expenditures vary with the level of gni. How big is that relationship?
Look at Coefficients, where it says 0.107 for gni.

That means that for every one-dollar increase in gni, we expect health expenditures to go up by about 11 cents.  It's always a one-point change in the independent variable and the number is what happens to the dependent. 

Because whole numbers are easier to talk about, multiply it by 10: For every 10-dollar increase in gni, we expect health expenditures to go up 1 dollar.

## Just a start...

You might end up adding more independent variables later. Keep in mind that your independent should not be highly correlated with each other and the more variables you add, the harder it is to explain to readers/listeners/viewers.

But if we wanted to add, say, unemployment to our model, we would use: 

```
ind_mod2 <- lm(formula = healthexp ~ gni+unemp, data=ind)
summary(ind_mod2)
```

Adding unemployment doesn't improve our model. The adjusted R squared is still .87 and unemploymment has a P value of .265, so it's not significant.


...wash, rinse, repeat


## Practice!

Use schools.csv

schools <- read_csv('schools.csv', col_names = TRUE)

describe(schools[5:19])

round(cor(schools[4:19], use="pairwise.complete.obs"),2)

What might be dependent and independent variables?

hist(schools$poorpct)
hist(schools$p_passed)

plot(schools$poorpct,schools$p_passed)

plot with regression line:

school_mod1 <- lm(formula = p_passed ~ poorpct, data = schools)
summary(school_mod1)

If you want to try a scatter plot with the plot line, first plot the variables (x-y), add labels and generate the line using your model. We plot poorpct, the independent variable on the X axis and p_passed on the Y axis. We'll use filled in circles for our plot points (pch=19) and we'll add a regression line.


plot(schools$poorpct, schools$p_passed, main="Scatterplot",
   xlab="poorpct", ylab="p_passed", pch=19) 
abline(school_mod1, col="red") # regression line (y~x)


## Predicted and residual values

If we want to add the "predicted value" to our data we can use the modelr package. That's the value that falls on the line. Given the poorpct, that's what a school "should" have. We also can add residuals, a fancy word for the difference between predicted and actual values.

schools_pr <- schools %>% add_predictions(school_mod1) %>% add_residuals(school_mod1)

We can take it one step further and standardize the residuals, so we know which ones are true outliers.

schools_pr$z_score <- as.numeric(scale(schools_pr$resid))


## Data for lata

R has lots of practice data sets preloaded for your analysis entertainment.
To see the list us this command:

```
data()
```
Let's practice with the very simple "cars" database. Step through these one at a time.

```
head(cars)
describe(cars)
cor(cars)
hist(cars$dist)
hist(cars$speed)
cars_model <- lm(formula = dist ~ speed, data = cars)
summary(cars_model)
```
Fancy plot:

```
plot(cars$speed, cars$dist, main="Scatterplot",
   xlab="Speed", ylab="Distance", pch=19) 
abline(cars_model, col="red") # regression line (y~x)
```
